subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Think about the last time the electricity in your home flickered on after dark. A single small bulb, or the glow of a phone screen, suddenly pushes back the night. In many homes across East Africa that light does not come from a distant power station at all - it comes from a solar panel on the roof charging a battery, which then powers lamps, a radio, and the charger for a mobile phone. Every one of those things works because of two invisible partners in nature that you will study in this unit: electricity and magnetism. They are so closely linked that scientists give them a single joined name, electromagnetism, and together they run almost every machine humans have ever built.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 8 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit you will be able to:
Explain what an electric current is and describe how charge flows around a complete circuit.
Define and use the words current, voltage (potential difference) and resistance correctly, with their units.
Draw and recognise simple circuit diagrams using the correct symbols for a cell, battery, bulb, switch, ammeter and voltmeter.
Compare how current and voltage behave in series and parallel circuits.
Measure current with an ammeter and voltage with a voltmeter, connecting each the correct way.
Describe the relationship between current, voltage and resistance, and use it in simple calculations.
Explain what a magnet is, describe magnetic fields and poles, and state the rules of attraction and repulsion.
Explain how an electromagnet works and how to make it stronger, and give real uses of electromagnets.
Describe the motor effect and how an electric motor turns electrical energy into movement.

concepts.title: What Is Electric Current?
concepts.explanation: Everything around you is made of tiny particles called atoms, and inside every atom are even smaller particles. Some of these carry a tiny amount of electric charge. In metals, some of these charged particles are free to move about, and when they all drift in the same direction along a wire, we have an electric current. So an electric current is simply the flow of electric charge. It is a little like water flowing through a pipe, except that instead of water it is charge that flows, and instead of a pipe it flows…
concepts.example: Not every material lets current pass. A conductor is a material that current flows through easily, because it has charged particles that are free to move. Metals such as copper, aluminium and iron are excellent…

concepts.title: Voltage, the Push Behind the Current
concepts.explanation: If current is the flow of charge, something must push that charge around the loop. That push is called voltage, and it comes from the cell or battery. The proper scientific name for voltage is potential difference, but in everyday use we simply say voltage. Voltage is measured in volts (V). Think of the cell as a pump and the current as water flowing round a loop of pipe. The pump does not create the water - it pushes it round. In the same way, the cell does not create charge - it provides the energy that pushes…
concepts.example: Inside a cell, stored chemical energy is turned into electrical energy that pushes the current. This is why batteries eventually go flat - the chemicals are used up, the voltage falls, and the push gets weaker until the…

concepts.title: Resistance, Slowing the Current Down
concepts.explanation: The third big idea is resistance. Resistance is how much a component slows down the current that tries to pass through it. Everything in a circuit has some resistance, even the wires, but some components resist much more than others. Resistance is measured in ohms. As the charged particles drift along, they keep bumping into the fixed particles of the material and are slowed down. A material or component that makes this harder has a high resistance; one that lets charge slip through easily has a low resistance.…
concepts.example: A longer wire has more resistance than a short one, because the charge has further to travel and more particles to bump into.

concepts.title: Circuits: Series and Parallel
concepts.explanation: Now that you know about current, voltage and resistance, you can understand how components are joined together. There are two main ways to build a circuit, and they behave very differently. Learning to tell them apart is a key skill of this unit. Scientists do not draw pictures of real bulbs and batteries every time - it would take too long and look untidy. Instead they use a set of agreed symbols to draw a neat circuit diagram. You should learn these common symbols:
concepts.example: A cell is drawn as one long thin line and one short thick line side by side (the long line is the positive end).

concepts.title: Measuring Current and Voltage
concepts.explanation: A real scientist does not just guess how much current flows - they measure it. There are two instruments you must know, and each is connected in its own special way. An ammeter measures the current in amps. Because the current has to pass through it to be counted, an ammeter is always connected in series - it becomes part of the single loop, so that all the current flows through it, like a gate that counts everything passing along the road. If you break the loop and slot the ammeter into the gap, it will read the…
concepts.example: A voltmeter measures the voltage across a component in volts. Voltage is a difference in push between two points, so a voltmeter is connected in parallel - across the component, touching the wire on each side of it,…

concepts.title: Magnets and Magnetic Fields
concepts.explanation: We now turn to the second half of the unit: magnetism. At first it may seem completely separate from electricity, but you will soon see that the two are deeply connected. Let us start with what a magnet is. A magnet is an object that can attract certain metals and that always has two poles, called the north pole and the south pole. The force of a magnet is strongest at its poles. There is one golden rule of magnets that you must know by heart:
concepts.example: This means two north poles push each other away, and two south poles push each other away, but a north pole and a south pole pull together. You can feel this yourself with two fridge magnets: turn them one way and they…

explorations.title: Building Your First Circuit (Battery, Wire and Bulb)
explorations.context: To build a complete circuit that lights a bulb, and to prove that current only flows when the loop is complete.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write: "I predict the bulb will light when ... and will go out when ..." Fill in the gaps with what you expect.
explorations.answer: What happened to the bulb when you made a gap in the loop? What does this tell you about a circuit? Explain why a switch is really just a controlled gap in a circuit. Why was the bulb brighter with two batteries? Use the word voltage in your answer. The wires are copper inside plastic. Why copper…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a complete circuit that lights a bulb, and to prove that current only flows when the loop is complete.

explorations.title: Series versus Parallel Bulbs
explorations.context: To compare how bulbs behave when wired in series (one loop) and in parallel (separate branches).
explorations.prompt: Predict: "I think two bulbs in series will be ... than two bulbs in parallel, and when I remove one bulb in each circuit ..."
explorations.answer: Which circuit gave brighter bulbs, series or parallel? Explain using the idea of shared versus full voltage. In which circuit did removing one bulb switch off the other? Why? Why are the lights in a house wired in parallel and not in series? In the series circuit, is the current the same through…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To compare how bulbs behave when wired in series (one loop) and in parallel (separate branches).

explorations.title: Make an Electromagnet (Battery, Nail and Wire)
explorations.context: To build a working electromagnet and to find out what makes it stronger.
explorations.prompt: Predict: "I think the nail will pick up ... paperclips, and adding more turns of wire will make it ..."
explorations.answer: What happened to the magnetism when you disconnected the battery? What does this tell you about an electromagnet? Did more turns of wire make the electromagnet stronger or weaker? Explain. Did using two batteries change the strength? Why? Why is an electromagnet more useful than a normal magnet for…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a working electromagnet and to find out what makes it stronger.

explorations.title: Magnetic or Not? Poles and Field Mapping
explorations.context: To find out which materials are magnetic, to test the rule of poles, and to map a magnetic field with a compass.
explorations.prompt: Predict: "I think a magnet will pick up ... but not ... , and when I bring two magnets together ..."
explorations.answer: Which objects were magnetic? What do they have in common? The copper coin and aluminium foil are metal but were not attracted. What does this tell you about metals and magnetism? What did you feel when two like poles met, and when two unlike poles met? State the rule of poles. What do your compass…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out which materials are magnetic, to test the rule of poles, and to map a magnetic field with a compass.

methods.title: Building Your First Circuit (Battery, Wire and Bulb)
methods.example: To build a complete circuit that lights a bulb, and to prove that current only flows when the loop is complete.
steps: Tape one wire to the flat negative (-) end of the battery, and touch its other end to the metal base of the bulb.
Tape a second wire to the bump on the positive (+) end of the battery, and touch its other end to the side of the bulb, so the bulb sits in a complete loop.
Observe: does the bulb light? You have made a complete circuit.
Now lift one wire away from the bulb to make a gap in the loop. Watch what happens to the bulb.
Reconnect it, then add a second battery in a line (positive of one to negative of the next) and watch the brightness.

methods.title: Series versus Parallel Bulbs
methods.example: To compare how bulbs behave when wired in series (one loop) and in parallel (separate branches).
steps: SERIES: Connect the two bulbs one after the other in a single loop with the battery, so the current must pass through both. Note how bright they are.
While the series circuit is lit, remove one bulb (or lift its wire). Watch what happens to the other bulb.
PARALLEL: Now re-wire so each bulb has its own loop back to the battery (two branches side by side). Note how bright they are compared with the series bulbs.
While the parallel circuit is lit, remove one bulb. Watch what happens to the other bulb.
Record everything in the table below.

methods.title: Make an Electromagnet (Battery, Nail and Wire)
methods.example: To build a working electromagnet and to find out what makes it stronger.
steps: Wind the wire neatly around the nail about 20 times, all in the same direction, leaving a length of wire free at each end.
Tape one bared end of the wire to the + end of the battery and the other bared end to the - end, so current flows through the coil. (Do this just before testing, and disconnect between tests.)
Bring the tip of the nail close to the pile of paperclips and count how many it picks up.
Disconnect the battery. Watch what happens to the paperclips - do they fall off?
Now wind about 20 MORE turns of wire onto the nail (about 40 in total), reconnect, and count the paperclips again.

methods.title: Magnetic or Not? Poles and Field Mapping
methods.example: To find out which materials are magnetic, to test the rule of poles, and to map a magnetic field with a compass.
steps: Touch the magnet to each object in turn. Sort them into two groups: attracted (magnetic) and not attracted.
If you have two magnets, bring two ends together, then turn one around and bring them together again. Feel the attraction and the repulsion.
Place a bar magnet on the paper and set the compass at several points around it. At each point, draw a small arrow showing the way the compass needle points.
Join up your arrows to draw the curved field lines running from one pole to the other.
Record which objects were magnetic and what you felt with the two magnets.

workedExamples.title: Current, Voltage and Resistance
workedExamples.prompt: Follow the reasoning in each example. In every one, notice how we use the rule voltage = current x resistance and the behaviour of series and parallel circuits.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1 - Finding the current Question: Khadija connects a bulb of resistance 3 ohms to a battery giving 6 V. What current flows through the bulb? Step 1 - Choose the right form of the rule. We want the current, so use current = voltage / resistance. Step 2 - Put in the numbers. current = 6 V / 3 ohms = 2. So the current is 2 A.

workedExamples.title: 1 - Finding the current
workedExamples.prompt: Question: Khadija connects a bulb of resistance 3 ohms to a battery giving 6 V. What current flows through the bulb?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Choose the right form of the rule. We want the current, so use current = voltage / resistance. Step 2 - Put in the numbers. current = 6 V / 3 ohms = 2. So the current is 2 A. Answer: A current of 2 amps flows through the bulb. Notice that if the resistance were larger, the same 6 V would push a smaller current. Worked Example 2 - Finding the voltage

workedExamples.title: 2 - Finding the voltage
workedExamples.prompt: Question: A current of 0.5 A flows through a component with a resistance of 4 ohms. What is the voltage across it?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Choose the right form of the rule. We want the voltage, so use voltage = current x resistance. Step 2 - Put in the numbers. voltage = 0.5 A x 4 ohms = 2. So the voltage is 2 V. Answer: The voltage across the component is 2 V. This is the push being used up driving the current through that component. Worked Example 3 - Series circuit voltage sharing

workedExamples.title: 3 - Series circuit voltage sharing
workedExamples.prompt: Question: Yusuf connects two identical bulbs in series to a 3 V battery. Roughly what voltage does each bulb get, and how does the current in the two bulbs compare?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Voltage. In a series circuit the voltage is shared between the components. Two identical bulbs share the 3 V equally, so each gets about 1.5 V. Step 2 - Current. In a series circuit the current is the same everywhere, so exactly the same current flows through both bulbs. Answer: Each bulb gets about 1.5 V, and the same current flows through both. Because each bulb has only half the battery's voltage, the two bulbs are dimmer than a single bulb would be. Worked Example 4 - Parallel circuit voltage

workedExamples.title: 4 - Parallel circuit voltage
workedExamples.prompt: Question: Halima now connects the same two bulbs in parallel across the 3 V battery. What voltage does each bulb get, and what happens if one bulb breaks?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Voltage. In a parallel circuit each branch gets the full battery voltage, so each bulb gets the whole 3 V and shines brightly. Step 2 - One bulb breaks. Each bulb has its own complete loop, so if one breaks, the other keeps its full voltage and stays lit. Answer: Each bulb gets the full 3 V and is bright, and if one bulb breaks the other still works. This is why homes are wired in parallel. Reading Comprehension - Light After Sunset in Garissa

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: In a series circuit, the current is: (a) different everywhere (b) the same everywhere (c) zero (d) only in one bulb
workedExamples.solution: (b) the same everywhere - in a series circuit there is only one path, so the current is equal at every point.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: In a parallel circuit, each branch gets: (a) no voltage (b) half the voltage (c) the full voltage (d) double the voltage
workedExamples.solution: (c) the full voltage - each parallel branch gets the whole battery voltage.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: An ammeter is connected: (a) in series (b) in parallel (c) across a bulb (d) outside the circuit
workedExamples.solution: (a) in series - the current must pass through the ammeter, so it goes in the loop.

practice.prompt: An electric current is: (a) a store of charge (b) the flow of electric charge (c) a kind of magnet (d) a type of voltage
practice.answer: (b) the flow of electric charge - that is the definition of a current.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Current is measured in: (a) volts (b) ohms (c) amperes (d) watts
practice.answer: (c) amperes - current is measured in amps (A); volts measure voltage and ohms measure resistance.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The push that drives current round a circuit is called: (a) resistance (b) voltage (c) current (d) charge
practice.answer: (b) voltage - voltage (potential difference) is the push from the cell.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A single dry cell (torch battery) gives about: (a) 1.5 V (b) 12 V (c) 230 V (d) 100 V
practice.answer: (a) 1.5 V - a single torch cell gives 1.5 V; the mains is around 230 V.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which material is the best conductor? (a) plastic (b) rubber (c) copper (d) glass
practice.answer: (c) copper - metals conduct well; plastic, rubber and glass are insulators.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In a series circuit, the current is: (a) different everywhere (b) the same everywhere (c) zero (d) only in one bulb
practice.answer: (b) the same everywhere - in a series circuit there is only one path, so the current is equal at every point.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In a parallel circuit, each branch gets: (a) no voltage (b) half the voltage (c) the full voltage (d) double the voltage
practice.answer: (c) the full voltage - each parallel branch gets the whole battery voltage.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: An ammeter is connected: (a) in series (b) in parallel (c) across a bulb (d) outside the circuit
practice.answer: (a) in series - the current must pass through the ammeter, so it goes in the loop.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A voltmeter is connected: (a) in series (b) in parallel (across a component) (c) in the loop (d) to one wire only
practice.answer: (b) in parallel (across a component) - a voltmeter measures the voltage across a component.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which metal is magnetic? (a) copper (b) aluminium (c) iron (d) gold
practice.answer: (c) iron - iron, steel, nickel and cobalt are magnetic; copper, aluminium and gold are not.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Two north poles brought together will: (a) attract (b) repel (c) do nothing (d) join
practice.answer: (b) repel - like poles repel; unlike poles attract.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: An electromagnet is magnetic: (a) all the time (b) never (c) only while current flows (d) only when cold
practice.answer: (c) only while current flows - switch off the current and an electromagnet loses its magnetism.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Building Your First Circuit (Battery, Wire and Bulb)
activities.materials: One or two 1.5 V batteries (torch cells).; A small torch bulb (or a small LED, with an adult's help).; Two or three pieces of insulated wire with the ends bared (an adult strips the plastic).; Sticky tape to hold the wire ends to the battery.
steps: Tape one wire to the flat negative (-) end of the battery, and touch its other end to the metal base of the bulb.
Tape a second wire to the bump on the positive (+) end of the battery, and touch its other end to the side of the bulb, so the bulb sits in a complete loop.
Observe: does the bulb light? You have made a complete circuit.
Now lift one wire away from the bulb to make a gap in the loop. Watch what happens to the bulb.
Reconnect it, then add a second battery in a line (positive of one to negative of the next) and watch the brightness.

activities.title: Series versus Parallel Bulbs
activities.materials: One or two 1.5 V batteries.; Two identical small bulbs (in holders if you have them, or held with tape).; Several pieces of wire with bared ends.; Sticky tape.
steps: SERIES: Connect the two bulbs one after the other in a single loop with the battery, so the current must pass through both. Note how bright they are.
While the series circuit is lit, remove one bulb (or lift its wire). Watch what happens to the other bulb.
PARALLEL: Now re-wire so each bulb has its own loop back to the battery (two branches side by side). Note how bright they are compared with the series bulbs.
While the parallel circuit is lit, remove one bulb. Watch what happens to the other bulb.
Record everything in the table below.

activities.title: Make an Electromagnet (Battery, Nail and Wire)
activities.materials: A large iron nail (steel nails work well too).; About one metre of thin insulated wire with the ends bared.; One or two 1.5 V batteries.; A small pile of steel paperclips or pins to pick up.; Sticky tape.
steps: Wind the wire neatly around the nail about 20 times, all in the same direction, leaving a length of wire free at each end.
Tape one bared end of the wire to the + end of the battery and the other bared end to the - end, so current flows through the coil. (Do this just before testing, and disconnect between tests.)
Bring the tip of the nail close to the pile of paperclips and count how many it picks up.
Disconnect the battery. Watch what happens to the paperclips - do they fall off?
Now wind about 20 MORE turns of wire onto the nail (about 40 in total), reconnect, and count the paperclips again.

activities.title: Magnetic or Not? Poles and Field Mapping
activities.materials: One or two magnets (fridge magnets or bar magnets).; A small compass (or use a magnetised needle floated on water).; A collection of small objects: iron nail, steel spoon, copper coin, aluminium foil, plastic ruler, wooden peg, glass marble.; A sheet of paper and a pencil.
steps: Touch the magnet to each object in turn. Sort them into two groups: attracted (magnetic) and not attracted.
If you have two magnets, bring two ends together, then turn one around and bring them together again. Feel the attraction and the repulsion.
Place a bar magnet on the paper and set the compass at several points around it. At each point, draw a small arrow showing the way the compass needle points.
Join up your arrows to draw the curved field lines running from one pole to the other.
Record which objects were magnetic and what you felt with the two magnets.

activities.title: Observe: Measuring Current and Voltage
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of measuring current and voltage around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Magnets and Magnetic Fields
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of magnets and magnetic fields around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: the meters. Ammeter = Along (series); Voltmeter = across (parallel). Repeat it until it sticks.

terms: Electric current
The flow of electric charge around a circuit; measured in amperes (A).
Voltage (potential difference)
The push from a cell that drives the current; measured in volts (V).
Resistance
How much a component slows the current; measured in ohms.
Cell
A single source of voltage using stored chemical energy (about 1.5 V).
Battery
Two or more cells joined together; their voltages add up.
Circuit
A complete loop of conductors that lets current flow.
Conductor
A material that lets current pass easily (copper, metal, the body).
Insulator
A material that does not let current pass (plastic, rubber, glass).
Series circuit
One single loop; same current everywhere, shared voltage.
Parallel circuit
Separate branches; each branch gets the full voltage.
Ammeter
Measures current in amps; connected in series.
Voltmeter
Measures voltage in volts; connected in parallel (across a component).

vocabulary.meaning: The flow of electric charge around a circuit; measured in amperes (A).
vocabulary.example: You cannot see an electric current, yet it lights a torch, rings a doorbell and carries a voice across a phone.

vocabulary.meaning: The push from a cell that drives the current; measured in volts (V).
vocabulary.example: Year 8 - Unit 9 - Lesson - Current, Voltage, Resistance, Circuits, Magnets and Electromagnets

vocabulary.meaning: How much a component slows the current; measured in ohms.
vocabulary.example: Year 8 - Unit 9 - Lesson - Current, Voltage, Resistance, Circuits, Magnets and Electromagnets

vocabulary.meaning: A single source of voltage using stored chemical energy (about 1.5 V).
vocabulary.example: Draw and recognise simple circuit diagrams using the correct symbols for a cell, battery, bulb, switch, ammeter and voltmeter.

vocabulary.meaning: Two or more cells joined together; their voltages add up.
vocabulary.example: In many homes across East Africa that light does not come from a distant power station at all - it comes from a solar panel on the roof charging a battery, which then powers lamps, a radio, and the charger for a mobile…

vocabulary.meaning: A complete loop of conductors that lets current flow.
vocabulary.example: A family that understands a simple circuit can wire up a solar lamp, work out why a torch has gone dark, and know never to touch a switch with wet hands.

vocabulary.meaning: A material that lets current pass easily (copper, metal, the body).
vocabulary.example: Current can only flow when there is a complete circuit - an unbroken loop of conductor leading from one end of a cell, through the wires and components, and back to the other end of the cell.

vocabulary.meaning: A material that does not let current pass (plastic, rubber, glass).
vocabulary.example: An insulator is a material that current cannot flow through, because its charged particles are locked in place.

vocabulary.meaning: One single loop; same current everywhere, shared voltage.
vocabulary.example: The current is the same everywhere in a series circuit.

vocabulary.meaning: Separate branches; each branch gets the full voltage.
vocabulary.example: A parallel circuit gives the current more than one path by splitting it into separate branches.

vocabulary.meaning: Measures current in amps; connected in series.
vocabulary.example: Draw and recognise simple circuit diagrams using the correct symbols for a cell, battery, bulb, switch, ammeter and voltmeter.

vocabulary.meaning: Measures voltage in volts; connected in parallel (across a component).
vocabulary.example: Draw and recognise simple circuit diagrams using the correct symbols for a cell, battery, bulb, switch, ammeter and voltmeter.

commonMistakes: Thinking the cell stores the electricity the bulb uses up.
Thinking current gets used up round the circuit.
Thinking bigger voltage always means bigger current.
Thinking an electromagnet stays magnetic like a normal magnet.

fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) the same everywhere - in a series circuit there is only one path, so the current is equal at every point.

fluency.prompt: In a parallel circuit, each branch gets: (a) no voltage (b) half the voltage (c) the full voltage (d) double the voltage
fluency.answer: (c) the full voltage - each parallel branch gets the whole battery voltage.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) the full voltage - each parallel branch gets the whole battery voltage.

fluency.prompt: An ammeter is connected: (a) in series (b) in parallel (c) across a bulb (d) outside the circuit
fluency.answer: (a) in series - the current must pass through the ammeter, so it goes in the loop.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (a) in series - the current must pass through the ammeter, so it goes in the loop.

fluency.prompt: A voltmeter is connected: (a) in series (b) in parallel (across a component) (c) in the loop (d) to one wire only
fluency.answer: (b) in parallel (across a component) - a voltmeter measures the voltage across a component.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) in parallel (across a component) - a voltmeter measures the voltage across a component.

fluency.prompt: Which metal is magnetic? (a) copper (b) aluminium (c) iron (d) gold
fluency.answer: (c) iron - iron, steel, nickel and cobalt are magnetic; copper, aluminium and gold are not.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) iron - iron, steel, nickel and cobalt are magnetic; copper, aluminium and gold are not.

fluency.prompt: Two north poles brought together will: (a) attract (b) repel (c) do nothing (d) join
fluency.answer: (b) repel - like poles repel; unlike poles attract.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) repel - like poles repel; unlike poles attract.

fluency.prompt: An electromagnet is magnetic: (a) all the time (b) never (c) only while current flows (d) only when cold
fluency.answer: (c) only while current flows - switch off the current and an electromagnet loses its magnetism.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) only while current flows - switch off the current and an electromagnet loses its magnetism.

realProblems.prompt: A family near Garissa lights their home with a solar panel and a battery. Explain where the electricity comes from during the day and during the night.
realProblems.answer: 1. During the day, the solar panel uses sunlight to provide voltage that pushes current to light the lamps and to charge the battery. During the night, the battery gives back its stored energy and pushes the current itself, so the lamps still work after dark.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. During the day, the solar panel uses sunlight to provide voltage that pushes current to light the lamps and to charge the battery. During the night, the battery gives back its stored energy and pushes the current itself, so the lamps still work after dark.

realProblems.prompt: Sagal's house is wired in parallel. Explain what this means and why it is better than wiring the house in series.
realProblems.answer: 2. Wired in parallel means each lamp and socket has its own branch back to the battery. This is better than series because each device gets the full voltage (so lamps are bright), each can be switched on or off on its own, and if one lamp fails the others keep working.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 2. Wired in parallel means each lamp and socket has its own branch back to the battery. This is better than series because each device gets the full voltage (so lamps are bright), each can be switched on or off on its own, and if one lamp fails the others keep working.

realProblems.prompt: A traveller in the savanna uses a compass to find north, and a family uses one to find the qibla. Explain how a small magnet can do this.
realProblems.answer: 3. A compass is a tiny magnet that is free to turn. It lines up with the Earth's magnetic field, so one end always swings round to point roughly north. From north the traveller can work out any direction, including the qibla for prayer.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3. A compass is a tiny magnet that is free to turn. It lines up with the Earth's magnetic field, so one end always swings round to point roughly north. From north the traveller can work out any direction, including the qibla for prayer.

realProblems.prompt: A scrapyard crane uses a giant electromagnet to move heaps of scrap iron. Explain why an electromagnet, not an ordinary magnet, is used.
realProblems.answer: 4. An electromagnet is magnetic only while the current flows, so the operator can switch it on to lift the scrap iron and off to drop it exactly where wanted. An ordinary magnet could never let go of the load, so it could not be used.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 4. An electromagnet is magnetic only while the current flows, so the operator can switch it on to lift the scrap iron and off to drop it exactly where wanted. An ordinary magnet could never let go of the load, so it could not be used.

realProblems.prompt: Idris reaches for a light switch with wet hands after washing. Explain, using the words conductor and current, why his mother tells him to dry his hands first.
realProblems.answer: 5. The human body is a conductor, and wet skin conducts even better, so touching a switch with wet hands makes it easy for a current to flow through him. Drying his hands first reduces this danger - a habit that is vital with the mains.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5. The human body is a conductor, and wet skin conducts even better, so touching a switch with wet hands makes it easy for a current to flow through him. Drying his hands first reduces this danger - a habit that is vital with the mains.

realProblems.prompt: A bulb of resistance 4 ohms is connected to a 12 V battery. Use the rule voltage = current x resistance to work out the current, showing your steps.
realProblems.answer: 1. Use current = voltage / resistance. current = 12 V / 4 ohms = 3 A. So a current of 3 amps flows through the bulb.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. Use current = voltage / resistance. current = 12 V / 4 ohms = 3 A. So a current of 3 amps flows through the bulb.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain what an electric current is and why a circuit must be a complete loop.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1. An electric current is the flow of electric charge around a circuit. The circuit must be a complete loop because charge can only flow if there is an unbroken path from one end of the cell, through the components, and back to the other end; any gap stops the current everywhere.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between voltage and current using the water-in-a-pipe picture.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2. Voltage is the push, like a pump driving water round a loop of pipe. Current is the flow, like the water actually moving. The cell provides the voltage that pushes the current (the charge) round the circuit.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is resistance? Name two things that increase the resistance of a wire.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3. Resistance is how much a component slows down the current. Resistance is increased by making a wire longer and by making it thinner (and some materials resist more than others).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: State the rule that links voltage, current and resistance.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4. Voltage = current x resistance. For a fixed voltage, more resistance means less current; more voltage (same resistance) means more current.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe how current and voltage behave in a series circuit.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5. In a series circuit the current is the same everywhere (one path), and the voltage is shared between the components, so bulbs are dimmer than one bulb alone.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe how current and voltage behave in a parallel circuit.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6. In a parallel circuit each branch gets the full voltage, so bulbs are bright, and each branch has its own loop, so one branch can fail without stopping the others.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit you will be able to:
I can explain what an electric current is and describe how charge flows around a complete circuit.
I can define and use the words current, voltage (potential difference) and resistance correctly, with their units.
I can draw and recognise simple circuit diagrams using the correct symbols for a cell, battery, bulb, switch, ammeter and voltmeter.
I can compare how current and voltage behave in series and parallel circuits.
I can measure current with an ammeter and voltage with a voltmeter, connecting each the correct way.
I can describe the relationship between current, voltage and resistance, and use it in simple calculations.
I can explain what a magnet is, describe magnetic fields and poles, and state the rules of attraction and repulsion.
